# Histogram Valued Data

Histogram valued data is, well, data that is represented by a histogram. There is a whole area of statistical research dedicated to what is called symbolic data analysis which includes histogram valued data and interval valued data among others. When we deal with data that are in non-standard "crisp" number form alternate methods are required. 

For histogram valued data, much of the analysis falls into a similar vain as functional dat analysis, since a histogram can be and often is represented as it's step function. This step function of histogram valued data is similar to the empirical CDF with large bin widths.

## Histogram Valued Analysis


# HistKit

HistKit is a Python library I have developed for working with histogram data. It provides the dataclasses of `Histogram` for histogram valued objects and `HistogramMatrix` for collections of histogram valued data. Along with the functions to create these objects, there are plotting, summary statistics, and transformation methods for the objects.

Here is an example of a histogram valued object in python using the Histogram dataclass.

::: {#00661db0 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import numpy as np
from histkit.hist import Histogram

# Create a histogram from raw data
data = np.random.normal(size=1000)
hist = Histogram.from_raw_data(data, bins=10)

# Print the histogram
print(hist)

# Get histogram properties
print(f"Mean: {hist.mean}")
print(f"Standard deviation: {hist.std}")

# Plot the histogram
# hist.plot(color='blue', edgecolor='black')
```
:::


This will output

```

          X                   p      
Bin_1     [ -3.06 ; -2.48 )          0.011   
Bin_2     [ -2.48 ; -1.9 )           0.033   
Bin_3     [ -1.9 ; -1.32 )           0.093   
Bin_4     [ -1.32 ; -0.742 )         0.16    
Bin_5     [ -0.742 ; -0.163 )        0.201   
Bin_6     [ -0.163 ; 0.415 )         0.222   
Bin_7     [ 0.415 ; 0.994 )          0.168   
Bin_8     [ 0.994 ; 1.57 )           0.081   
Bin_9     [ 1.57 ; 2.15 )            0.024   
Bin_10    [ 2.15 ; 2.73 ]            0.007   

mean = -0.163...         std = 0.988...
Mean: -0.163...
Standard deviation: 0.988...
```

More information can be found on the official github repo [HistKit](https://github.com/nathenbyford/HistKit)
